title: JavaScript Control Flow Knowledge Check
description: Check your understanding of branching, loops, switch statements, break, and continue.
author: DailyCoder

options.text: Only when its condition is exactly the string "true"
Only after an else block runs

questions.explanation: An if block runs when its condition evaluates to a truthy value.

questions.prompt: In an `if...else if...else` chain, how many matching branches run?

options.text: None unless there is no else block

questions.explanation: JavaScript checks the chain from top to bottom and runs only the first matching branch.

questions.prompt: Why is `if (quantity)` risky when `quantity` can be `0`?

options.text: 0 is truthy, so it always runs
0 is falsy, so valid zero values may be treated as missing
0 throws a TypeError in if statements
quantity is automatically converted to a string

questions.explanation: 0 is falsy, so a broad truthy/falsy check can accidentally reject a valid value.

questions.prompt: Which loop is usually best when you know the exact number of iterations or are using an array index?
questions.explanation: A for loop is commonly used for known counts and index-based iteration.

questions.prompt: What is the correct condition for looping over every item in `items` by index?
questions.explanation: Array indexes start at 0 and the last index is length - 1, so use i < items.length.

options.text: A loop that runs exactly once
A loop whose condition never becomes false
A loop inside a switch statement

questions.explanation: An infinite loop keeps running because its stopping condition is never reached.

questions.prompt: When is a `while` loop usually a good choice?

options.text: When the number of iterations depends on a changing condition
Only when there is a switch statement
When the loop must never stop

questions.explanation: while loops are useful when the number of iterations is not known ahead of time.

questions.prompt: What is the key difference between `while` and `do...while`?

options.text: do...while always runs at least once
while always runs at least once

questions.explanation: do...while checks its condition after the body runs, so it runs at least once.

questions.prompt: What does `switch` use to match a case?
questions.explanation: switch cases match using strict equality-like behavior, so value and type must match.

questions.prompt: What happens in a `switch` when a matching case has no `break`?

options.text: JavaScript may fall through and run the next case
The switch restarts from the top
The default case always runs first

questions.explanation: Without break or return, execution falls through into the following case statements.

options.text: When checking ranges or complex conditions
When matching one value against exact strings
When you need a default case

questions.explanation: if...else is better for ranges, compound conditions, and checks involving multiple variables.

questions.prompt: What does `break` do inside a loop?

options.text: Converts the condition to false permanently

questions.explanation: break exits the loop completely and continues with the code after the loop.

questions.prompt: What does `continue` do inside a loop?

options.text: Skips the rest of the current iteration and moves to the next one

questions.explanation: continue skips the rest of the current iteration without stopping the entire loop.

questions.prompt: Which are good practices for control flow? (Select all that apply.)

options.text: Use curly braces for if blocks
Make sure loops move toward a stopping condition
Prefer deeply nested if statements whenever possible

questions.explanation: Curly braces, strict equality, and reachable stopping conditions make control flow safer. Deep nesting usually hurts readability.

questions.prompt: In nested loops, what does a normal unlabeled `break` exit?
questions.explanation: A normal break exits the nearest enclosing loop. Labels are needed to break out of an outer loop directly.
